incidents.victim: Mobile phone users in Greater Toronto Area
incidents.vector: Rogue cellular 'SMS blaster' base stations driven around the city impersonated legitimate telecom towers to push smishing lures spoofing banks and government, bypassing carrier spam filters.
incidents.impact: Tens of thousands of phones connected to the rogue system and roughly 13 million network disruptions were recorded, including interference with 911 access; Toronto police arrested three suspects in Canada's first SMS-blaster case.

incidents.victim: FinTech and cryptocurrency firms with macOS-heavy executives
incidents.vector: Attackers took over trusted Telegram accounts to send fake Zoom/Teams meeting invites; the 'ClickFix' lure tricked targets into pasting commands that downloaded a malicious macOS binary loading an infostealer dubbed macrasv2.
incidents.impact: Stealer harvested browser credentials, cookies, macOS Keychain entries, and SaaS session tokens and exfiltrated them via Telegram, enabling follow-on takeover of corporate email, SaaS, and crypto accounts at targeted firms.

incidents.victim: US federal civilian agency (unnamed) and other Cisco ASA/Firepower operators
incidents.vector: APT actor exploited Cisco ASA/FTD zero-days CVE-2025-20333 and CVE-2025-20362 to deploy the FIRESTARTER backdoor, which achieves persistence by manipulating the Cisco Service Platform mount list and survives firmware patching and reboot.
incidents.impact: CISA found the backdoor still active on a federal Firepower device six months after initial compromise, harvesting VPN credentials, certificates, and private keys; Emergency Directive 25-03 was updated to force all FCEB agencies to snapshot Cisco device memory, and UK NCSC co-sealed the report.

incidents.victim: Global critical infrastructure, telecom, and government networks
incidents.vector: Chinese state-linked contractors operate large-scale covert botnets of compromised SOHO routers, IoT devices, firewalls, and NAS devices that are rented out to Chinese intrusion sets to route reconnaissance, malware delivery, C2, and exfiltration through deniable residential IPs.
incidents.impact: A 12-nation joint advisory (CISA, NSA, FBI, NCSC-UK, Australia, Canada, Germany, Netherlands, New Zealand, Japan, Spain, Sweden) warned that rapid IP rotation causes 'IOC extinction' and defeats static blocklists, giving Chinese operators cheap, scalable pre-positioning access to US and allied critical infrastructure.

incidents.victim: Mongolian government ministries
incidents.vector: Custom backdoors abused legitimate cloud services — Slack, Discord, Microsoft Outlook email drafts, and file.io — for command-and-control and exfiltration, blending malicious traffic into normal enterprise usage.
incidents.impact: Long-running espionage campaign active since November 2023 compromised at least 12 systems in one Mongolian institution with dozens more suspected, harvesting sensitive government communications before public disclosure.

incidents.victim: American victims of Cambodia-based 'pig butchering' investment scams
incidents.vector: Scam-compound operators in Cambodia used social-engineering and romance lures staffed by trafficked labor to direct victims to bogus crypto investment platforms, with proceeds laundered through US-accessible crypto rails.
incidents.impact: OFAC sanctioned Cambodian Senator Kok An plus 28 people and entities; DOJ seized 503 fraudulent crypto-investment domains and a messaging app used to recruit trafficked workers, and the State Department offered a $10 million reward for further information.

incidents.victim: Vercel (and a subset of its hosted Next.js customers)
incidents.vector: Initial compromise traced to a Context.ai employee infected with Lumma infostealer in February 2026; stolen OAuth tokens and API keys were reused to traverse Vercel's systems and decrypt customer environment variables.
incidents.impact: Attackers exfiltrated access keys, source code, and database material tied to a small number of Vercel customers and began attempting to sell the data; Mandiant is leading incident response while Vercel analyzes roughly a petabyte of logs, and the fallout is still expanding.

incidents.victim: Web3 developers and cryptocurrency users worldwide
incidents.vector: Fake LinkedIn recruiters from AI-generated front companies (including a shell firm registered in Mexico) sent candidates malicious 'coding assessments' that installed the BeaverTail, OtterCookie, and InvisibleFerret stealers targeting password managers and the macOS Keychain.
incidents.impact: About $12 million in cryptocurrency was drained from 26,584 wallets across 2,726 infected systems in Q1 2026; researchers tied the campaign to 31 operators organized into six teams.

incidents.victim: Over 200 organizations across government, education, healthcare, and manufacturing
incidents.vector: Affiliates gain initial access through exposed internet-facing services, deploy the SystemBC proxy, pivot to domain controllers, and abuse Active Directory Group Policy to detonate ransomware network-wide, including an ESXi variant that evades most AV.
incidents.impact: A newcomer ransomware-as-a-service crew claimed 202 victims last quarter — second only to Qilin — with 1,570+ infected hosts observed in its SystemBC botnet and a 90% payout split to affiliates fueling rapid growth.

incidents.victim: Mastodon (flagship mastodon.social instance)
incidents.vector: Sustained distributed denial-of-service flood against the flagship instance.
incidents.impact: The largest Mastodon server was knocked offline, following a similar DDoS campaign against Bluesky days earlier; no data compromise reported but service was degraded for thousands of users.

incidents.victim: Five US companies that hired a ransomware negotiation firm
incidents.attacker: Insider colluding with criminal gang — ransomware
incidents.vector: A former DigitalMint ransomware negotiator leaked confidential negotiation details to a ransomware crew so the attackers could hold out for higher ransom payments from his own clients.
incidents.impact: Third US cyber professional to plead guilty for colluding with ransomware operators; clients ended up paying inflated ransoms and the case further erodes trust in the ransomware-negotiation industry.

incidents.victim: Smaller Dutch hosting and internet service providers
incidents.vector: Chinese operators compromised edge routers at Dutch hosting providers as part of a broader campaign attributed to three Chinese tech companies working on behalf of Beijing.
incidents.impact: Dutch military intelligence (MIVD) confirmed router-level intrusions at multiple smaller Dutch ISPs in 2025 but said attackers did not pivot into internal networks; disclosure is part of Dutch warnings that China has reached cyber parity with the US.

incidents.victim: Twilio, LastPass, DoorDash, Mailchimp, and US crypto investors (historical 2022–2024 activity)
incidents.vector: SMS phishing against tech-company employees and SIM-swap attacks against cryptocurrency holders, operated by an English-speaking cybercrime crew.
incidents.impact: Tyler Robert Buchanan ('Tylerb'), age 24, pleaded guilty in US court to wire-fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft, admitting theft of more than $8 million in cryptocurrency and facing up to 22 years in prison.

incidents.victim: 200+ organizations including foreign ministries, law enforcement, and email providers; 18,000+ consumer/SOHO routers
incidents.vector: Exploited known vulnerabilities in older Mikrotik and TP-Link SOHO routers to rewrite DNS settings, redirecting Microsoft Office OAuth lookups to attacker-controlled servers that silently harvested authentication tokens — no malware required on endpoints.
incidents.impact: Peaking in December 2025, the operation gave Russian military intelligence mass access to Microsoft 365 session tokens across more than 18,000 compromised routers and 200+ high-value organizations; Microsoft, Lumen Black Lotus Labs, and NCSC-UK publicly disclosed the activity this week.

incidents.victim: Israeli water treatment and desalination operators
incidents.vector: Purpose-built OT malware designed to identify Israeli IP ranges and water-utility software and then manipulate pump-pressure and chlorine dosing levels to dangerous settings.
incidents.impact: Malware was intended to poison drinking water in Tel Aviv and Haifa but failed due to a XOR-encoding bug in its geolocation check; no operational impact occurred, but the payload shows continued Iranian intent to sabotage Israeli water infrastructure.

incidents.victim: Kelp DAO
incidents.vector: Exploited a vulnerability in Kelp DAO's smart contracts to withdraw funds as rsETH restaked-Ether tokens.
incidents.impact: Roughly $292 million in crypto assets were stolen, making it the largest cryptocurrency heist of 2026 so far; Kelp DAO is working with exchanges and analytics firms to trace the funds.

incidents.victim: Rhea Finance
incidents.vector: Attacker primed fake token pools over several days, then exploited a margin-trading feature vulnerability to route protocol funds to attacker-controlled wallets.
incidents.impact: Approximately $18.4 million in assets were drained from Rhea Finance's platform.

incidents.victim: City of Tallahassee, Florida
incidents.vector: Incident specifics undisclosed; Leon County disconnected its network from the city and officials used 'contamination' language consistent with a ransomware intrusion.
incidents.impact: City of Tallahassee took much of its IT network offline, knocking out multiple public-facing city websites and services while investigation and recovery continue.

incidents.victim: Bluesky
incidents.vector: Sophisticated distributed denial-of-service flood against Bluesky's infrastructure.
incidents.impact: Prolonged intermittent outage lasting nearly a day on April 15, followed by further DDoS attempts throughout the week; no data compromise reported.

incidents.victim: Grinex (sanctioned Russian crypto exchange)
incidents.vector: Wallet compromise; technical details not publicly disclosed. Grinex publicly blamed 'Western intelligence agencies' without providing evidence.
incidents.impact: Roughly $13 million (about 1 billion rubles) was stolen from Grinex, which shut down operations; the exchange had been OFAC-sanctioned in 2025 for laundering ransomware payments.

incidents.victim: Hallmark Cards
incidents.vector: Data was stolen from Hallmark's Salesforce tenant during the 2025 wave of Salesforce-targeting extortion attacks and leaked publicly after the company refused to pay.
incidents.impact: Personal information on 1.7 million Hallmark customers was published online by the extortion group; Hallmark declined to pay the ransom demand.
